g.text: 1. Real-time log viewing for debugging (latency < a few seconds)
2. Regex-based querying on real-time logs for live troubleshooting
3. Tar-archived log export for analytics teams covering 24–48 hours
2. Low Query Latency: Regex queries should return results in <2s
3. Data Retention: Logs must be stored at full fidelity for 48 hours
GET /logs/stream?service={}&region={}&starttime={} --- more like a SSE, web sockets, gRPC
Each record processed by Flink = 2.78 million logs/sec
If tagging adds latency ≤ 50ms, Flink must scale horizontally
~10–20 Flink workers with autoscaling, depending on complexity
If Flink crashes, it replays logs from Kafka starting
from last committed offset per partition.
So checkpointing + Kafka retention = reliable replay
Inverted index: On message (for regex search)
Bloom filter: On message to fast-fail queries with unlikely matches
Assuming 1 log line = 1 KB (fair average with metadata, structured fields):
Kafka best practices: partition size ~50–100 MB/sec
So, partitions = 2.78 GB/sec ÷ 100 MB/sec = ~28–30 Kafka partitions (at minimum)
Add buffer for scaling, say 50–100 partitions
If we flush logs hourly for archive:
Each tar file job processes 10 TB and uploads to S3
log.segment.bytes=1GB # For faster compaction and segment delete
log.retention.bytes=-1 # Don't restrict by size, only by time
Enforce time bounded queries from UI
For larger time bounds, accept job
and return tar download link later
